auto_merge.commit_message:
This was left out of https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7424

* This PR excludes subscripts with ellipsis `tuple[int, ...]`
* This PR excludes type inference when initializing from tuple literals: `a = (1, 'bar')`
auto_merge.commit_title: Implement support for subscripted types of tuple (GH-7704)

repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
This PR needs some love but main spirit is there. Some notes:

* This PR will not support subscript with ellipsis `tuple[int, ...]`
* This PR will not support inference when initialized: `a = (1, 'bar')`

TODO:
* [x] make CI green
* [x] tests with negative scenarios
* [x]  https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7704#discussion_r3293964487
* [x] https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7704#discussion_r3293977702
* [x] https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7704#discussion_r3294163830

-    def infer_indexed_type(self):
+    def infer_indexed_type(self, index=None):
         container_type = self.get_container_type()
+        if container_type.is_pytuple_type:
+            if index is None:
+                return self.get_subscripted_spanning_type()
+            if isinstance(index, int):
+                return self.get_subscripted_type(index)
+            return py_object_type

comment_data.body: BTW, we could evaluate the reduced spanning type once, when instantiating the type, and store it away in an attribute, say, `.common_item_type`, but for all container types. That way, we'd also have a common way to access it in one place (with the twist that `infer_iterator_type()` would still need a special case for dicts).

Here we can do something even more smarter e.g.:
* `tuple[int, int, int]` does have uniform elements
* `tuple[int, ...]` will have uniform elements too (when supported)

comment_data.body:
Hmm now I am thinking. slicing inference can be done safely only when we support `...` in tuples since:

```python
l: tuple[int, int, int] = (1, 2, 3)
l[i:j] # this slice must have type either tuple or tuple[int, ...] since we don't know values of i,j
```

comment_data.body: This should use `infer_sequence_item_type()` (in this module). That handles `None` values and starred values correctly.

I don't think this should be necessary. `self.base.infer_type()` must only return a specialised (i.e. subscripted) type if its elements are of uniform type, in which case any slice will also be uniform. If the base container is not known to be homogeneous, it must return the plain type without further specialisation. In either case, it should be correct to pass the base type through. The rest is just a potential optimisation for known slices with only uniform item types.

EDIT: The same applies to the equivalent change in `IndexNode`.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -5609,6 +5613,10 @@ def infer_type(self, env):
             return bytes_type
         elif base_type.is_pyunicode_ptr:
             return unicode_type
+        elif base_type.supports_container_type and not base_type.has_uniform_element_type:
+            # slicing a container with non-uniform element types (tuple[int, str]) depends on the slice indices,
+            # so we can't infer a more specific type here.
+            return base_type.get_container_type()

comment_data.body: Please don't use this pattern. It makes it too easy to miss the doc string overwrite when reading the test function itself, and it also requires jumping back and forth across a long piece of code to compare the output of Python and Cython. And then, we are testing Cython's type system here, not Python's. The test should directly show the expected output for the Cython code, with adaptations for Python only where needed, to make it work there as well.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -341,6 +397,34 @@ def test_dict_with_subscript():
     print(cython.typeof(c))
     print(c)
 
+def test_assignment_tuple_with_subscript():
+    """
+    >>> test_assignment_tuple_with_subscript()
+    int int
+    int int
+    int int
+    5 5 5
+    6 6 6
+    """
+    a: tuple[cython.int, cython.int] = (5, 6)
+    b: tuple = a
+    c: tuple[cython.float, cython.float] = b
+    print(cython.typeof(a[0]), cython.typeof(a[1]))
+    print(cython.typeof(b[0]), cython.typeof(b[1]))
+    print(cython.typeof(c[0]), cython.typeof(c[1]))
+    print(a[0], b[0], c[0])
+    print(a[1], b[1], c[1])
+
+if cython.compiled:
+    test_assignment_tuple_with_subscript.__doc__ = """

comment_data.body:
You mean a compile time error, rather than the runtime error that you'd get either way? I'm not sure it should be an error. It might just be a warning case.

… and I only just noticed that we already reject the other float to int assignments here. I'm not sure if that's a bug or feature. I understand that we cannot allow assignments of Python `float` to Python `int`, they are different objects. But C `float` to C `int`, as declared here? Why shouldn't that work?

comment_data.body:
There is no need for the walrus operator here. Just split the if-elif-cases into separate if-statements and do whatever you need to do between them.

If you then reverse the first two conditions, and put them into nested if-blocks, you can avoid calling the potentially costly `infer_type()` if it's not needed and also bring the assignment and usage of `sequence_type` closer together.

I also suggest assigning `self.iterator.sequence` to a local variable to make its various usages easier to spot in the long condition.

             item_type = PyrexTypes.remove_cv_ref(item_type, remove_fakeref=True)
             return item_type
-        elif (sequence_type := self.iterator.sequence.infer_type(env)).supports_container_type and \
-                (iterator_type := sequence_type.infer_iterator_type()):
+        elif (
+            (sequence_type := self.iterator.sequence.infer_type(env)).supports_container_type and
+            not (
+                self.iterator.sequence.is_sequence_constructor or
+                self.iterator.sequence.is_dict_literal or
+                self.iterator.sequence.is_set_literal
+            ) and
+            (iterator_type := sequence_type.infer_iterator_type())

comment_data.body: It's not using `infer_sequence_item_type()` but instead needlessly duplicating a part of its logic.
If the interfaces of the two methods disagree about the representation of "don't know", then we need to make both consistent. You can map `py_object_type` to `None` in `get_common_item_type`.

comment_data.body:
There are certainly cases where assignment expressions make the code more compact and/or readable, but I consider it a feature that comes with downsides. It always _increases_ the code complexity by mixing a simple assignment with a simple condition into a lengthy  and less readable expression. If it can easily be avoided, as in this case, I think it's worth keeping the code simple.
```suggestion
            if sequence_type.supports_container_type:
                iterator_type = sequence_type.infer_iterator_type():
                if iterator_type:
                    return iterator_type
```

             return item_type
-        elif (sequence_type := self.iterator.sequence.infer_type(env)).supports_container_type and \

comment_data.body: This method looks fine now.

I now think that my assumption was not correct. I was thinking of literal lists/set/etc., not of tuples. For the latter, the specialised type is the correct result of type inference, so this case is needed.

commit.message: Implement support of subscripted types of tuple

commit.message: Fix type inference of slicing for tuples
Fix type inference of literals, use existing sequence infer mechanism
Try to run without none type

repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

milestone.description:
Main features:
- Pattern matching
- Generics / subscripted container types
- `@cython.collection_type()`
- `restrict`
- more vectorcalls
- more thread safety
- smaller modules, more shared code
- remove Py3.8 support, keep Py3.9+

title: Implement support of subscripted types of tuple
